title: Hospital Fire on a 28-Bed Med-Surg Unit (Smoke from a Patient Room from an Electrical Bedside Heating-Pad Fire): Leading the RACE Response (Rescue, Alarm, Confine, Extinguish/Evacuate), Using a Class ABC Extinguisher with PASS, SHUTTING OFF Oxygen to the Affected Zone, NEVER Using Elevators, HORIZONTAL Evacuation to the Next Smoke Compartment Behind Fire Doors First, Prioritizing Patients (AMBULATORY First → Partially Mobile → Bedbound Last), CLOSED-LOOP Role Assignment Under Incident Command, and Accounting for Every Patient and Staff Member
subtitle: Hospital fires are uncommon but high-consequence — even a small fire produces SMOKE (the leading cause of injury/death) and demands an immediate, choreographed team response. The universal mnemonic for the nurse is RACE: RESCUE anyone in immediate danger from the fire/smoke (move them to safety first); ALARM — pull the nearest fire alarm pull station and call the facility's emergency code (often 'Code Red') so engineering, security, and the fire department are activated; CONFINE/CONTAIN — CLOSE all patient room doors, corridor doors, and fire doors (modern hospital corridors are divided by FIRE DOORS into SMOKE COMPARTMENTS specifically designed to slow fire/smoke spread); SHUT OFF OXYGEN to the affected zone if safely possible (oxygen is an OXIDIZER — it ACCELERATES fires) and coordinate with respiratory therapy for any ventilator-dependent patient; turn off non-essential equipment; and finally EXTINGUISH if the fire is small, contained, and safe to fight with the appropriate extinguisher — OR EVACUATE if it is not. EXTINGUISHER use follows PASS: PULL the pin, AIM at the BASE of the fire (not the flames), SQUEEZE the handle, and SWEEP side to side. CLASS matters: Class A = ordinary combustibles (paper/wood/cloth); Class B = flammable liquids (oil/grease/solvents); Class C = ELECTRICAL fires (NEVER use water on a live electrical fire — risk of electrocution and spread); Class K = kitchen/cooking grease. Most hospitals use ABC dry-chemical extinguishers that cover A/B/C; an electrical bedside heating pad is a Class C fire — use ABC or CO2 — and unplug the device first if safely possible. EVACUATION priority is high-yield NCLEX: HORIZONTAL evacuation FIRST — move patients laterally to the NEXT SMOKE COMPARTMENT behind closed fire doors (often safer and faster than going down stairs); vertical evacuation (down stairs, to a lower floor or outside) is reserved for fires that overwhelm horizontal containment. NEVER use ELEVATORS during a fire — they can become smoke chimneys, lose power, or open onto the fire floor. PATIENT-EVACUATION ORDER: evacuate AMBULATORY patients FIRST (they require minimal staff and clear the unit fastest, freeing staff to help others), then PARTIALLY MOBILE patients (wheelchairs, walkers, light assistance), then BEDBOUND and CRITICAL patients LAST (the most staff- and equipment-intensive). Patients in immediate fire/smoke danger are RESCUED first regardless of mobility. Use sheets/blankets/evacuation chairs/stair chairs for non-ambulatory patients per facility plan. ROLE ASSIGNMENT/LEADERSHIP: the CHARGE RN at the scene immediately functions within the hospital INCIDENT COMMAND SYSTEM — assigning closed-loop roles to staff (one for rescue, one to activate the alarm/call the code, one to close doors and shut off oxygen with RT coordination, one to lead horizontal evacuation, one to keep a manifest/account for patients and staff, and one to brief arriving engineering/security/fire department); communicating via SBAR; and delegating BY SCOPE (RN retains triage, FHR/medical assessment, and medication decisions; LVN can move stable patients, close doors, account for patients; UAP/support can move equipment, push beds, push wheelchairs, control corridor traffic, escort visitors). Don't manage alone; don't freelance. COMMUNICATION: notify charge/house supervisor, switchboard, RT, security; use overhead code/PA per facility (code Red). DOCUMENTATION: name of patients evacuated/where/when, what was done with oxygen/equipment, fire location, who was notified. PREVENTION: smoke-free hospital, restrict patient personal heating pads/space heaters/candles (high-risk items), enforce oxygen safety (no flames/sparks within 10 ft, no petroleum products on the patient using O2, no smoking, pulse-equipment inspection), maintain unobstructed corridors and fire doors, weekly extinguisher checks, evacuation drills. NURSING ROLE: at the first sign of smoke/alarm, lead the RACE sequence; close doors; shut off oxygen to the affected zone (RT for ventilated patients); use PASS with the correct extinguisher type for the fire (no water on electrical); evacuate HORIZONTALLY first; NEVER use elevators; evacuate AMBULATORY-first; assign closed-loop roles; account for every patient and staff member; document and debrief.

patient.name: Charge RN — Med-Surg 4-East
patient.code: Mixed (most full code; honor each patient's directive); goal = immediately lead the RACE response to a Class C electrical fire in a patient room, contain spread, evacuate per fire plan (horizontal first, never elevators, ambulatory first), assign closed-loop roles under incident command, and account for every patient and staff member
patient.admitDate: Routine evening on a 28-bed medical-surgical unit (mixed acuity — ambulatory, partially mobile, and several bedbound patients including one on continuous oxygen). At 1942 a smoke odor and visible smoke are noted from Room 412; the patient's personal electric heating pad has shorted and ignited the bedding (a Class C electrical fire). The patient is at the bedside, alert and ambulatory. Smoke is beginning to enter the corridor. The CHARGE RN must immediately lead the RACE response and the unit evacuation while assigning closed-loop roles to RN/LVN/UAP staff under hospital incident command.
patient.room: Med-surg 4-East, point of origin Room 412; adjacent rooms 410/411/413/414 occupied; fire doors and stair access available; engineering, security, RT, and the fire department being activated

vitals.hr: Charge RN at scene; team mobilizing
vitals.temp: Visible smoke from Room 412 (Class C electrical fire from a patient's personal heating pad); patient is alert and ambulatory; immediate priority = RESCUE the patient from the room (and any adjacent at-risk patients) before anything else

vitals.hr: Patient rescued from Room 412 to safe area
vitals.temp: Patient moved to a safe area down the corridor; charge RN PULLS the nearest fire-alarm pull station and calls 'Code Red Room 412' via the facility number; engineering, security, RT, house supervisor, and fire department notified

vitals.bp: Smoke spread slowing behind closed fire doors
vitals.temp: All patient room doors and corridor fire doors CLOSED; OXYGEN to the affected zone SHUT OFF (oxygen is an oxidizer that accelerates fire); RT coordinates the one ventilator-dependent patient (kept on portable O2/manual support); non-essential equipment turned off; corridor cleared

Heating pad UNPLUGGED (safe to do so); ABC dry-chemical extinguisher used per PASS — PULL pin, AIM at base, SQUEEZE, SWEEP; small Class C fire contained; NEVER use water on an electrical fire; if extinguishing were not safe, the team would skip directly to evacuation

vitals.time: 1947 — EVACUATE (horizontal first; ambulatory first; no elevators)
vitals.bp: Patients moving to next smoke compartment
vitals.rr: Stairwells used as needed; NO elevators
vitals.spo2: Bedbound patients moved last with staff
vitals.temp: HORIZONTAL evacuation to the next smoke compartment behind closed fire doors; AMBULATORY patients first, then partially mobile, then bedbound/critical last (evacuation chairs/sheets for non-ambulatory); ELEVATORS are NOT used (smoke chimney/power risk); rooms cleared with door tags or marks

Manifest reconciled — every patient and staff member accounted for; status briefed to fire department/incident command using SBAR; family liaison engaged; documentation of timeline/actions complete; team debrief planned

labs.v: 1942 on a 28-bed medical-surgical unit (mixed acuity — ambulatory, partially mobile, several bedbound patients, including one on continuous oxygen and one on a ventilator). Smoke and a smell of burning are noticed from Room 412 — the patient's personal electric heating pad has shorted and ignited the bedding (a CLASS C ELECTRICAL fire). The room's patient is alert and ambulatory; smoke begins to enter the corridor. The charge RN must lead the immediate response. The universal mnemonic is RACE: (R) RESCUE anyone in immediate danger from fire/smoke (the room patient is moved out first, and any adjacent patient at risk); (A) ALARM — pull the nearest fire-alarm pull station + call the facility's emergency code ('Code Red Room 412') so engineering, security, respiratory therapy, the house supervisor, and the fire department are activated; (C) CONFINE/CONTAIN — close all patient room doors and corridor fire doors (smoke compartments slow spread), shut off oxygen to the affected zone (oxygen accelerates fire) with RT coordinating any ventilated patient, and turn off non-essential equipment; (E) EXTINGUISH or EVACUATE — if the fire is SMALL, CONTAINED, and safe, extinguish with the correct extinguisher type using PASS; otherwise EVACUATE per the fire plan. This is both a clinical emergency and a LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION challenge requiring immediate role assignment under the hospital Incident Command System.
labs.r: RACE = Rescue (patient in danger first) → Alarm (pull station + Code Red + call switchboard so engineering/security/RT/house sup/fire department mobilize) → Confine/Contain (close ALL doors + fire doors; SHUT OFF OXYGEN to zone — O2 accelerates fire; RT coordinates vented pt; turn off non-essential equipment) → Extinguish or Evacuate (small/safe → PASS extinguisher; otherwise evacuate). Class C electrical fire. RN role: lead RACE + assign roles + account for everyone.

labs.n: Extinguisher use (PASS) + fire CLASSES + oxygen-safety nuance
labs.v: Use of a fire extinguisher follows PASS: PULL the pin; AIM at the BASE of the fire (not at the flames); SQUEEZE the handle; SWEEP side to side. Stand with an exit at the back, and never let the fire get between the user and the exit. EXTINGUISHER CLASSES: Class A = ordinary combustibles (paper/wood/cloth/trash); Class B = flammable liquids (oil/grease/solvents); Class C = ELECTRICAL fires (live electricity); Class K = kitchen/cooking grease. NEVER use water on a Class C (electrical) fire — risk of electrocution and fire spread — use CO2 or ABC dry chemical. Most hospitals stock ABC dry-chemical extinguishers that cover Classes A/B/C. The fire here is Class C from a personal electric heating pad — UNPLUG the device first if safely possible (removes the electrical source), then extinguish with ABC or CO2. EXTINGUISH only if the fire is SMALL, CONTAINED, you have a clear exit, and you are trained; otherwise EVACUATE and let the fire department handle it. OXYGEN-SAFETY nuance: oxygen does not burn but is an OXIDIZER that dramatically accelerates fire — SHUT OFF oxygen to the affected zone whenever safely possible; if a patient depends on oxygen (ventilator, severe hypoxemia), RT coordinates continued support (portable cylinder/manual bagging/relocation). Routine oxygen safety teaching applies year-round: no open flames or sparks within ~10 feet, no smoking, no petroleum products on the patient (Vaseline/oil-based ointments), inspect equipment, and educate patients/visitors.
labs.r: PASS = Pull pin / Aim BASE / Squeeze / Sweep; keep exit at back. CLASSES: A ordinary, B flammable liquids, C ELECTRICAL (NEVER water), K kitchen grease. Hospitals usually use ABC dry-chem (covers A/B/C); electrical fire → unplug source if safe + ABC/CO2. Extinguish ONLY if small/contained/clear exit/trained — otherwise evacuate. OXYGEN = oxidizer that accelerates fire → shut off to affected zone if safe; RT coordinates ventilated patient. RN role: correct extinguisher + oxygen safety.

labs.n: EVACUATION rules — horizontal first, no elevators, ambulatory-first priority
labs.v: Hospital fire evacuation is HIERARCHICAL: (1) HORIZONTAL evacuation FIRST — move patients laterally to the NEXT SMOKE COMPARTMENT behind closed fire doors (corridors are designed in compartments specifically to allow this); this is often safer and faster than moving down stairs. (2) VERTICAL evacuation — down stairs to a lower floor or out of the building — only when horizontal containment is insufficient. (3) NEVER use ELEVATORS during a fire — elevators can become smoke chimneys, lose power, or open directly onto the fire floor. PATIENT-EVACUATION ORDER (high-yield NCLEX): evacuate AMBULATORY patients FIRST (they clear the unit fastest with minimal staff, then staff are freed to help others), then PARTIALLY MOBILE (wheelchairs, walkers, light assistance), then BEDBOUND and CRITICAL patients LAST (most staff- and equipment-intensive). EXCEPTION: anyone in IMMEDIATE danger from the fire/smoke is RESCUED first regardless of mobility (that's the R in RACE). For non-ambulatory patients use sheets/blankets to drag, evacuation chairs, stair-descent chairs, or wheelchairs per the facility plan. Mark or tag cleared rooms (closed door + visible mark) so search teams know they are empty. Stage evacuated patients at a designated safe area; keep a manifest and account for every patient and staff member at handoff. Do NOT return to the area for personal belongings.
labs.r: EVACUATION: (1) HORIZONTAL FIRST → next smoke compartment behind fire doors; (2) VERTICAL down stairs only as needed; (3) NEVER elevators. ORDER: AMBULATORY first → partially mobile → bedbound/critical LAST; EXCEPTION = anyone in immediate fire/smoke danger is RESCUED first regardless of mobility. Tools: sheets/blankets, evacuation/stair chairs, wheelchairs per plan. Tag/mark cleared rooms. Stage at safe area + manifest + account for everyone. Don't return for belongings. RN role: prioritize + sequence + account.

labs.n: Leadership, role assignment, incident command, communication + Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: Hospital fires are managed within the INCIDENT COMMAND SYSTEM (ICS). The charge RN at the scene functions as a unit-level leader until the incident commander (often the house supervisor, then security/engineering, then fire department) takes overall command. The charge RN immediately ASSIGNS CLOSED-LOOP ROLES — using direct, named instructions and required readbacks — so multiple time-critical tasks happen in parallel: one staff member rescues the room patient; one pulls the alarm and calls the code; one closes all doors and shuts off oxygen to the zone (with RT coordinating any ventilator patient); one leads horizontal evacuation of ambulatory patients to the next compartment; one prepares evacuation chairs/sheets for non-ambulatory patients; one (often a unit clerk) maintains a PATIENT MANIFEST and accounts for visitors; one (often UAP) controls corridor traffic and clears equipment from the hallway; one briefs arriving engineering/security/fire/RT and serves as runner. COMMUNICATION: notify the charge/house supervisor, switchboard (overhead Code Red), RT, security; use SBAR for handoffs; do NOT manage alone and do NOT freelance. DELEGATE BY SCOPE: the RN retains triage and complex clinical judgment; LVN moves stable patients, closes doors, helps with extinguishers, accounts for patients; UAP/support staff move equipment, push beds/wheelchairs, control traffic, escort visitors, run for supplies. DOCUMENTATION: capture the timeline — time of alarm, who was notified and when, which patients were evacuated where, what was done with oxygen/equipment, location of every patient and staff member, and handoff to incident command. After the event lead a DEBRIEF. PREVENTION/AWARENESS: hospital fire-prevention culture — restrict patient personal heating pads/space heaters/candles (high-risk items); enforce oxygen safety (no open flames/sparks within ~10 ft, no smoking, no petroleum-based products on patients using O2); inspect equipment; keep corridors and fire doors clear; weekly extinguisher checks; regular evacuation drills. PERTINENT NEGATIVES (correct emergency practice): the charge RN did NOT use the elevators; did NOT use water on the electrical fire; did NOT leave oxygen running in the affected zone; did NOT evacuate the bedbound/critical patients first; did NOT prop fire doors open for ease; did NOT return for belongings; and did NOT manage the response alone. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: immediate recognition + RACE sequence; closed fire doors + smoke compartments; oxygen shut off; correct extinguisher (PASS, ABC/CO2 — no water on electrical); horizontal evacuation first; no elevators; ambulatory-first prioritization; closed-loop role assignment; SBAR communication; full manifest/accountability; documentation + debrief; and prevention/drill culture.
labs.r: ICS at the scene: charge RN leads until command transfers. CLOSED-LOOP role assignment: rescue / alarm + code / close doors + shut O2 (RT for vent) / lead horizontal evacuation / prep evacuation chairs/sheets / manifest + account for everyone / clear corridor / brief arriving teams + runner. SBAR handoffs. DELEGATE BY SCOPE: RN triage/clinical judgment; LVN move stable pts + doors + extinguishers; UAP move equipment/wheelchairs/traffic/visitors. Document timeline. Debrief. Negatives confirm correct practice (no elevators, no water on electrical, O2 OFF, ambulatory first, fire doors closed, don't go back for belongings, don't manage alone). Protective: recognition + RACE + closed fire doors + O2 off + PASS w/ right class + horizontal first + no elevators + ambulatory first + closed-loop roles + SBAR + manifest + documentation + drills.

nursesNote: 1942 — Charge RN, Med-Surg 4-East (28 beds; mixed acuity incl. ventilator pt + several O2 patients). Smoke + smell of burning from Room 412 — patient's personal electric heating pad shorted and ignited bedding = CLASS C ELECTRICAL fire. Patient alert/ambulatory; smoke entering corridor. Charge RN led RACE response: (R) RESCUED the room patient to a safe area down the corridor; (A) PULLED the nearest fire-alarm pull station + called 'Code Red Room 412' via switchboard → engineering/security/RT/house supervisor/fire department activated; (C) CONFINED/CONTAINED — ALL patient room doors + corridor FIRE DOORS closed; OXYGEN to the affected zone SHUT OFF (O2 = oxidizer that accelerates fire); RT coordinated the ventilator-dependent patient (kept on portable O2 + manual support per plan); non-essential equipment off; corridor cleared; (E) EXTINGUISHED — heating pad UNPLUGGED (safe), ABC dry-chemical extinguisher used per PASS — PULL pin / AIM at BASE / SQUEEZE / SWEEP (NO WATER on Class C electrical fire); small fire contained — if not safe to extinguish, would have proceeded straight to evacuation. EVACUATION: HORIZONTAL first to the next smoke compartment behind closed fire doors (no elevators ever — smoke chimney/power risk); AMBULATORY patients first → partially mobile → bedbound/critical last; evacuation chairs/sheets for non-ambulatory; cleared rooms tagged; staged at safe area; manifest reconciled — every patient and every staff member accounted for. LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION (ICS): closed-loop named role assignment — rescue, alarm + code, close doors + O2 off (RT for vent), horizontal evacuation lead, evacuation-chair prep, patient/visitor manifest, corridor/equipment control, runner/brief arriving teams; SBAR handoffs; delegate by scope (RN triage/clinical judgment; LVN move stable patients + doors + extinguishers + account; UAP equipment/wheelchairs/traffic/visitors). Communication: charge/house supervisor + switchboard + RT + security + family liaison. NEGATIVES (correct practice): did NOT use elevators, did NOT use water on the electrical fire, did NOT leave O2 running in the zone, did NOT evacuate the bedbound first, did NOT prop fire doors open, did NOT return for belongings, did NOT manage alone. Outcome: small Class C fire contained; full unit evacuated horizontally without injury; every patient and staff member accounted for; SBAR handoff to fire department/incident commander; documentation of timeline/actions complete; team debrief planned.
providerOrders: EMERGENCY: Code Red on Med-Surg 4-East, Room 412 (Class C electrical fire from a patient's personal heating pad); activate hospital Incident Command System

RACE: Rescue the room patient; pull the fire alarm and call Code Red; close all patient room doors and corridor fire doors; shut off oxygen to the affected zone with RT coordinating any ventilator-dependent patient; turn off non-essential equipment

EXTINGUISH (small/contained/safe): unplug the source if safely possible; use an ABC dry-chemical or CO2 extinguisher per PASS (Pull/Aim at base/Squeeze/Sweep); do NOT use water on an electrical fire; if not safe to extinguish, evacuate and let the fire department manage the fire
EVACUATE: horizontal first to the next smoke compartment behind closed fire doors; vertical evacuation down stairs only as needed; NEVER use elevators

EVACUATION ORDER: ambulatory patients first → partially mobile → bedbound/critical last; rescue any patient in immediate fire/smoke danger first regardless of mobility; use sheets/blankets, evacuation chairs, and stair-descent chairs per the facility plan

LEADERSHIP/DELEGATION: assign closed-loop named roles — rescue, alarm/code, close doors + oxygen off (with RT for ventilated patients), lead horizontal evacuation, prepare evacuation chairs, maintain a patient and staff manifest, control corridor/equipment/traffic, escort visitors, runner/briefer for arriving engineering/security/fire department; do not freelance and do not manage alone
COMMUNICATION: notify house supervisor, switchboard (overhead Code Red), respiratory therapy, security; SBAR handoffs to incident command and to the fire department; family liaison engaged

DELEGATE BY SCOPE: RN retains triage and complex clinical judgment; LVN moves stable patients, closes doors, helps with extinguishers, supports the manifest; UAP/support move equipment and wheelchairs, control corridor traffic, and escort visitors

ACCOUNTABILITY/DOCUMENTATION: tag or mark cleared rooms; reconcile a full manifest of all patients and staff members at the safe area; document the timeline — time of alarm, notifications, evacuations, oxygen and equipment actions, and handoff to command

AFTERCARE: post-incident debrief; reassess affected patients and provide reassurance; coordinate cleanup with engineering/environmental services; report to risk management and complete an event report

PREVENTION: enforce the policy against personal heating pads/space heaters/candles in patient rooms; reinforce oxygen safety (no open flames/sparks within ~10 ft, no smoking, no petroleum-based products on patients using oxygen); inspect extinguishers weekly and conduct regular evacuation drills

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters at the moment the fire is recognized?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Smoke and the smell of burning from a patient room — fire response must begin immediately using the RACE sequence
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — smoke is the leading cause of injury/death in fires; RACE is the universal nursing response. RN role: initiate RACE.

opts.text: The fire is Class C (electrical, from a personal heating pad) — never use water; use an ABC or CO2 extinguisher and unplug the source if safe
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — using water on an electrical fire risks electrocution and spread. RN role: correct extinguisher type.

opts.text: Oxygen is an oxidizer that accelerates fire — shut it off to the affected zone if safely possible and have RT coordinate any ventilated patient
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — oxygen makes a small fire much worse. RN role: oxygen shutoff + RT coordination.

opts.text: Close all patient room doors and corridor fire doors to contain smoke; evacuate horizontally to the next smoke compartment first
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — smoke compartments are designed to slow spread; horizontal evacuation is the safest first move. RN role: confine + horizontal first.

opts.text: NEVER use elevators during a fire (smoke chimney/power loss/may open on the fire floor); use stairs only when vertical evacuation is necessary
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — elevators are unsafe in fires. RN role: stairs only.

opts.text: Evacuate AMBULATORY patients first, then partially mobile, then bedbound/critical last — except anyone in immediate fire/smoke danger is rescued first regardless of mobility
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — ambulatory-first frees staff fastest; the exception is the R in RACE. RN role: evacuation order.

opts.text: Use the corridor elevator to move bedbound patients down to a lower floor quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + DANGEROUS. Elevators are never used during a fire; use stairs and evacuation chairs after horizontal options.

opts.text: Spray water from a sink/fire hose onto the burning electric heating pad
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Water on a live electrical (Class C) fire risks electrocution and spread; unplug if safe and use ABC/CO2.

opts.text: RACE structures the response: Rescue, Alarm, Confine/Contain, Extinguish or Evacuate
opts.rat: CORRECT — the universal response mnemonic. Foundation for the sequence.

opts.text: PASS describes correct extinguisher use: Pull the pin, Aim at the base, Squeeze, Sweep
opts.rat: CORRECT — extinguisher technique. Foundation for safe small-fire suppression.

opts.text: Class A = ordinary combustibles; Class B = flammable liquids; Class C = electrical (never water); Class K = cooking grease — hospitals usually stock ABC dry-chemical extinguishers
opts.rat: CORRECT — fire-class principles. Foundation for choosing the right extinguisher.

opts.text: Oxygen accelerates fire as an oxidizer — shut off oxygen to the affected zone if safely possible, with RT coordinating any ventilator-dependent patient
opts.rat: CORRECT — oxygen-safety principle. Foundation for containment.

opts.text: Evacuation is horizontal to the next smoke compartment FIRST; vertical (down stairs) only as needed; NEVER use elevators
opts.rat: CORRECT — evacuation hierarchy and the no-elevators rule. Foundation for safe movement.

opts.text: Patient evacuation order is ambulatory first → partially mobile → bedbound/critical last, with rescue of anyone in immediate danger regardless of mobility
opts.rat: CORRECT — evacuation order. Foundation for resource-efficient evacuation.

opts.text: Bedbound and critical patients should always be evacuated first because they are sickest, even if the corridor is filling with smoke
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Bedbound/critical patients require the most staff and equipment; evacuating them first leaves ambulatory patients trapped and ties up rescuers. Ambulatory first, with immediate-danger patients rescued regardless of mobility.

opts.text: Propping fire doors open during evacuation makes it easier to move patients and is acceptable
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Fire doors must be CLOSED to maintain smoke compartments; propping them open lets smoke/fire spread.

opts.text: RESCUE the patient(s) in immediate danger from the fire/smoke (and any adjacent at-risk patient)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Removing patients from immediate danger comes first; it is the R in RACE. RN role: rescue first.

opts.text: ALARM — pull the nearest fire-alarm pull station and call the facility Code Red so engineering, security, RT, and the fire department are activated
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. Activating the system mobilizes the full response; do it without leaving the immediate scene unattended. RN role: alarm + code.

opts.text: CONFINE/CONTAIN — close all patient room doors and corridor fire doors, shut off oxygen to the affected zone (with RT for ventilated patients), and turn off non-essential equipment
opts.rat: #3 — Slows fire/smoke spread and removes fire's fuel/accelerant. RN role: confine + oxygen off.

opts.text: EXTINGUISH if small/contained/safe (ABC or CO2 with PASS, no water on electrical) OR EVACUATE per the plan (horizontal first, no elevators, ambulatory first)
opts.rat: #4 — Choose extinguishment vs evacuation based on safety; either way, execute the correct sequence. RN role: PASS or evacuate.

opts.text: Assign closed-loop roles, maintain a patient/staff manifest, brief incident command via SBAR, and document the timeline
opts.rat: #5 — Leadership, accountability, and the record sustain a safe response. RN role: roles + manifest + SBAR + documentation.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this fire response.

opts.text: Rescue any patient in immediate fire/smoke danger first (then proceed to alarm/contain/extinguish or evacuate)
opts.rat: Removes the most immediate threat to life. RN role: rescue first.

opts.text: Pull the fire-alarm pull station and call the facility Code Red to mobilize engineering/security/RT/fire department
opts.rat: Activates the full response system. RN role: alarm + code.

opts.text: Close all patient room and corridor fire doors and shut off oxygen to the affected zone, with RT coordinating any ventilator-dependent patient
opts.rat: Confines smoke and removes fire's accelerant. RN role: confine + oxygen off.

opts.text: Use an ABC or CO2 extinguisher per PASS on a small contained Class C electrical fire, after unplugging the source if safely possible
opts.rat: Correct extinguisher class and technique. RN role: PASS with right class.

opts.text: Evacuate horizontally to the next smoke compartment first; use stairs (never elevators) for vertical evacuation if needed
opts.rat: Hierarchical evacuation that protects patients and staff. RN role: horizontal first + no elevators.

opts.text: Evacuate ambulatory patients first, then partially mobile, then bedbound/critical last — with anyone in immediate danger rescued first regardless of mobility
opts.rat: Frees staff fastest and protects the most lives. RN role: evacuation order.

opts.text: Assign closed-loop named roles, keep a patient and staff manifest, brief incident command via SBAR, and document the timeline
opts.rat: Leadership and accountability sustain a coordinated response. RN role: roles + manifest + SBAR + documentation.

opts.text: Use the corridor elevator to move bedbound patients down to a lower floor
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — elevators are never used during a fire.

opts.text: Spray water from a sink or fire hose onto the burning electric heating pad
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — water on an electrical (Class C) fire risks electrocution and spread; use ABC or CO2 and unplug the source.

opts.text: Leave oxygen running in the affected zone so patients are not bothered by the change
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — oxygen accelerates fire; shut it off to the affected zone with RT coordinating ventilated patients.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of the nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Rescue the patient(s) in immediate fire/smoke danger to a safe area
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Remove patients from immediate threat. RN role: rescue.

opts.text: Pull the fire-alarm pull station and call the facility Code Red (engineering, security, RT, house supervisor, fire department)
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Activate the system to mobilize the full response. RN role: alarm + code.

opts.text: Close all patient room and corridor fire doors and shut off oxygen to the affected zone (RT for ventilated patients); turn off non-essential equipment
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Confine smoke and remove the accelerant. RN role: confine + oxygen off.

opts.text: Extinguish the small contained electrical fire with an ABC/CO2 extinguisher using PASS (unplug source first if safe); if not safe to extinguish, evacuate
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Choose extinguishment vs evacuation based on safety. RN role: PASS or evacuate.

opts.text: Evacuate per the plan (horizontal first; no elevators; ambulatory first → partially mobile → bedbound last) with closed-loop roles, a full manifest, SBAR handoffs, and documentation
opts.rat: #5 — Closing. Coordinated evacuation and accountability complete the response. RN role: evacuate + lead + account + document.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify as APPROPRIATE or INAPPROPRIATE.

opts.text: The charge RN led RACE — rescued the patient, pulled the alarm, called Code Red, closed fire doors, shut off oxygen with RT coordinating the ventilated patient, and contained the small fire with an ABC extinguisher using PASS
opts.rat: Correct sequence with the right extinguisher class and oxygen-safety coordination. Model response.

opts.text: Evacuation was horizontal to the next smoke compartment first, no elevators were used, and ambulatory patients were moved first with bedbound/critical patients moved last by staff and evacuation chairs
opts.rat: Hierarchical, resource-efficient, life-safety evacuation. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Closed-loop named roles were assigned (rescue, alarm/code, doors/oxygen, evacuation lead, manifest, corridor control, runner); SBAR was used to brief incident command; every patient and staff member was accounted for
opts.rat: Coordinated leadership with full accountability. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Timeline, notifications, evacuation locations, oxygen/equipment actions, and handoffs were documented; a team debrief was scheduled
opts.rat: Documentation and debrief complete the response. Correct trajectory.

opts.text: Alternative — water was sprayed on the burning electric heating pad and the corridor elevator was used to move bedbound patients to a lower floor
opts.rat: ⚠️ Water on a Class C fire risks electrocution/spread, and elevators are never used during a fire. Demonstrates the two most dangerous mistakes.

opts.text: Alternative — oxygen was left running in the affected zone for the comfort of the patients while the fire grew quickly
opts.rat: ⚠️ Oxygen accelerates fire; shutting it off to the zone (with RT coordination for ventilated patients) is essential.

opts.text: Alternative — bedbound/critical patients were evacuated first, leaving ambulatory patients trapped in the corridor while staff were tied up moving beds
opts.rat: ⚠️ Bedbound/critical patients require the most staff and equipment; evacuating them first leaves others at risk. Ambulatory-first frees staff fastest.

opts.text: Alternative — fire doors were propped open during the evacuation to make moving beds easier, and smoke spread through the unit
opts.rat: ⚠️ Fire doors must be closed to maintain smoke compartments. Demonstrates why doors are kept closed.

opts.text: Alternative — the charge RN tried to manage the response alone and did not pull the alarm or assign roles; the response was disorganized and a patient was left behind
opts.rat: ⚠️ Solo, role-less response misses tasks and patients. Demonstrates why the alarm is pulled and closed-loop roles are assigned under incident command.

opts.text: After the event the team debriefed; the unit reinforced its policy against personal heating pads/space heaters and reviewed oxygen-safety teaching
opts.rat: Prevention/learning embedded after the response. Sustains future safety.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Safety and Infection Control, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
